mapping.a_element: Blume-Capel model under bond randomness exhibiting a second-order phase transition from a first-order regime in the pure system
mapping.b_element: 8-state Potts model on random lattices (Voronoi/Delaunay) retaining a first-order phase transition despite quenched randomness

mapping.a_element: quenched bond randomness altering the order of the phase transition (first to second order)
mapping.b_element: quenched coordination number randomness (random lattices) not altering the order of the phase transition (remains first order)

mapping.a_element: critical exponents (ν, β/ν) characterizing the new universality class under bond randomness
mapping.b_element: absence of critical exponents due to retained first-order transition under random lattices

mapping.a_element: Wang-Landau algorithm for entropic sampling in dominant energy subspaces
mapping.b_element: Monte Carlo simulations for studying phase transitions

mapping.a_element: microsegregation and connectivity spin densities explaining transition temperature increase
mapping.b_element: no analogous mechanism described for the Potts model on random lattices

brief_justification: While both papers study the effect of quenched randomness on phase transitions in two-dimensional lattice models, their core mechanisms diverge: Paper A demonstrates a change in the order of the transition (first to second order) with new critical exponents under bond randomness, whereas Paper B shows that quenched coordination number randomness does not alter the first-order nature of the transition. The underlying machinery (universality class alteration vs. retention of first-order behavior) is fundamentally different.
